rationale: The citing paper uses Lee and Labroo (2004) as a key theoretical benchmark for the standard fluency-to-positive-evaluation effect, including explicit discussion of its conceptual-fluency finding. It then argues that this pattern does not fully characterize judgments for well-established brands, proposing that metacognitive difficulty can reduce perceived understanding and increase openness to new cues. This role goes beyond a background mention because it directly structures the paper's framing, hypotheses, and interpretation of contributions.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Lee and Labroo (2004)'s fluency account but adds boundary conditions and mechanism-level qualification. It proposes that for established brands, disfluency can trigger understanding-restoration motives and increase influence of new claims, refining the prior fluency-brand evaluation link.
